Name: Test-First Gate
Exact Quote: Don’t write a line of new code unless you first have a failing automated test.
Intent: Let need (a failing test) drive production code; prevent speculative implementation under pressure.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Eliminate Duplication
Exact Quote: Eliminate duplication.
Intent: Continuously remove duplication so the design stays flexible and change-friendly.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Organic Design via Feedback
Exact Quote: You must design organically, with running code providing feedback between decisions
Intent: Let small, running increments steer design; avoid big up-front design commitments made under uncertainty.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Developer-Owned Tests
Exact Quote: You must write your own tests, since you can’t wait twenty times a day for someone else to write a test
Intent: Keep feedback loops in the developer’s hands so cadence doesn’t break under delivery pressure.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: High Cohesion, Loose Coupling
Exact Quote: Your designs must consist of many highly cohesive, loosely coupled components, just to make testing easy
Intent: Shape design so small parts are testable and changeable; resist entanglement that slows teams later.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Red: Write a Failing Test
Exact Quote: Red—write a little test that doesn’t work, perhaps doesn’t even compile at first
Intent: Make the next learning step explicit; keep scope tiny and observable.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Green: Make It Work Quickly
Exact Quote: Green—make the test work quickly, committing whatever sins necessary in the process
Intent: Prioritize a working state fast; postpone elegance until the refactor step.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Refactor: Remove the Duplication You Created
Exact Quote: Refactor—eliminate all the duplication created in just getting the test to work
Intent: Pay back design debt immediately so speed doesn’t collapse later.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Five-Step Rhythm
Exact Quote: 1. Quickly add a test
Intent: Keep a repeatable micro-routine that prevents drifting into large, untested changes.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Run Tests to See the New One Fail
Exact Quote: 2. Run all tests and see the new one fail
Intent: Confirm the test can fail for the right reason; avoid false confidence.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Change in Tiny Doses
Exact Quote: 3. Make a little change
Intent: Reduce risk by making the smallest production change that moves toward green.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Run Tests to See Everything Succeed
Exact Quote: 4. Run all tests and see them all succeed
Intent: Return to a known-good state before expanding scope or adding more behavior.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Refactor Before Moving On
Exact Quote: 5. Refactor to remove duplication
Intent: Make cleanup a required step, not “extra time if we have it.”
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Small Increments per Test
Exact Quote: How each test can cover a small increment of functionality
Intent: Keep each test narrowly focused so progress continues even when time is short.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Ugly Changes Are Acceptable (Temporarily)
Exact Quote: How small and ugly the changes can be to make the new tests run
Intent: Optimize for feedback first; rely on refactoring to restore structure.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Run Tests Very Often
Exact Quote: How often the tests are run
Intent: Sustain confidence and pace by keeping the feedback loop tight.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Many Tiny Refactor Steps
Exact Quote: How many teensy tiny steps make up the refactorings
Intent: Prefer frequent, small refactors over rare “big cleanups” that get postponed under deadlines.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Start Small
Exact Quote: Start small or not at all.
Intent: Lower the activation energy; start with the simplest case to avoid stalling or overbuilding.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Design the Best API First
Exact Quote: When we write a test, we imagine the perfect interface for our operation.
Intent: Use tests to shape the public interface from the user’s perspective, before committing internals.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Tests as an Outside-In Story
Exact Quote: We are telling ourselves a story about how the operation will look from the outside.
Intent: Keep focus on observable behavior and usage, not internal mechanisms.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: No Human Judgment in Results
Exact Quote: every bit of human judgment has to be taken out of the evaluation of the results.
Intent: Make tests unambiguous so teams don’t rationalize failures under pressure.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Assert Specifically
Exact Quote: Be specific--assertNotNull(result) can be satisfied by almost any code.
Intent: Prevent weak tests that “go green” without pinning down behavior.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Fake It to Regain Motion
Exact Quote: I write the first test, I make it work some ugly way
Intent: Allow temporary hard-coding to restore progress, then tighten with more tests and refactoring.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Eliminate Test↔Code Duplication
Exact Quote: There is duplication between the test and the code.
Intent: Treat duplicated knowledge across tests and code as coupling; remove it to keep changes cheap.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Triangulate: Abstract Only with Multiple Examples
Exact Quote: Only abstract when you have two or more examples.
Intent: Avoid premature generalization; let repeated evidence force abstraction.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: TDD Manages Fear
Exact Quote: Test-driven development (TDD) is a way of managing fear during programming.
Intent: Use fast feedback to keep risk and anxiety from driving big-bang decisions.
Source with Author name: Kent Beck — Test-Driven Development by Example

Name: Nano-Cycle Granularity
Exact Quote: The three laws are the nano-cycle of TDD.
Intent: Keep work at near line-by-line scale so “just a bit more code” doesn’t balloon under deadlines.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — The Cycles of TDD (Clean Coder Blog)

Name: 3 Laws: Failing Test First
Exact Quote: You must write a failing test before you write any production code.
Intent: Force demand-driven coding; prevent speculative implementation when time feels tight.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — The Cycles of TDD (Clean Coder Blog)

Name: 3 Laws: Minimal Test
Exact Quote: You must not write more of a test than is sufficient to fail, or fail to compile.
Intent: Avoid over-specifying behavior early; keep the next step small and focused.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — The Cycles of TDD (Clean Coder Blog)

Name: 3 Laws: Minimal Production Code
Exact Quote: You must not write more production code than is sufficient to make the currently failing test pass.
Intent: Prevent “extra” implementation that becomes brittle and expensive to maintain.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — The Cycles of TDD (Clean Coder Blog)

Name: Micro-Cycle: Fail → Pass → Clean
Exact Quote: Clean up the mess you just made.
Intent: Treat cleanup as part of the cycle so structure doesn’t degrade when delivery pressure rises.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — The Cycles of TDD (Clean Coder Blog)

Name: Refactor Continuously (Not Later)
Exact Quote: Refactoring is a continuous in-process activity, not something that is done late (and therefore optionally).
Intent: Block the common failure mode: postponing refactoring until it never happens.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — The Cycles of TDD (Clean Coder Blog)

Name: FIRST: Test Quality Constraints
Exact Quote: Tests should be Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-Validating, and Timely.
Intent: Make the test suite runnable and trustworthy enough that teams keep using it under time pressure.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — Clean Code (excerpted/quoted)

Name: Test Code Is First-Class
Exact Quote: Test code is just as important as production code.
Intent: Keep tests maintainable so they don’t become a drag and get skipped during delivery crunches.
Source with Author name: Robert C. Martin — quoted in Tyler Hawkins (Better Programming, Medium)
